text: Hey guys, it's Dr. Mario Leyva. I have a subtotal cholecystectomy that I recently did. I apologize for the video as it started recording after I decompressed it. The video will show some general concepts and my general approach to these types of cases. Some of the stuff I show you may be controversial or not necessarily standard, but this is kind of how I do it and I hope you enjoy.

Many times when you're doing these cases, your anatomy is disrupted. You don't know exactly where certain structures begin or end. So try to get a feel of the land where you think the duodenum is, where you think the colon is. The liver should be fairly visible. In general, anatomy as best as you can tell. And keep that in the back of your heads as you start doing your dissection. This may change, but at least you have boundaries on where potentially safe dissection is and where hazardous type dissection may lie. Use your Firefly and use it often. As in this case, at least early on, It doesn't seem like it's helpful as the gallbladder doesn't light up. I can't see the bile ducts However, it's actually giving me a lot of information. It's giving me information as to where the liver it begins and ends It's giving me a constant reassessment if there's any leakage of bile and It can tell me if some bile ducts are creeping up on the dissection and so forth So use it and use it often

The surgeon carefully manipulates the gallbladder to prevent spillage of indocyanine green (ICG) dye, using a vessel sealer to address the thick gallbladder wall and minimize bleeding. The focus is on maintaining a clean surgical field as the dissection approaches the ductal structures inferiorly.

The surgeon continues to follow the path of the ICG, using suction to remove bile and ICG to locate the entrance of the cystic duct into the lumen, which will guide the subsequent dissection and closure.

The surgeon approaches the cystic duct entrance, using a V-lock suture to close the gallbladder remnant area. Firefly cholangiogram and visual assessments are employed to ensure no leakage. A pedicle to falciform flap is created, followed by the application of Vista seal fiber and glue. A drain is placed at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon navigates the challenging transition to close the top part of the gallbladder remnant, using the back wall tissue when necessary. The thick gallbladder wall is visible, and the surgeon carefully places sutures to seal the area, preparing for the application of a falciform flap or Vista seal. Attention is given to potential structures in the medial region.
